points.text: Map current session cookie usage and identify all authentication entry points.
Introduce JWT issuance behind a feature flag while keeping session fallback active.
Use short-lived access tokens (e.g., 15 min) and store refresh tokens securely (httpOnly, sameSite).
Sign JWTs with a strong algorithm like RS256 and set up regular key rotation.
Validate JWT signature and expiration on every request; reject invalid or expired tokens.
Include only minimal, non‑sensitive claims in JWTs; never embed passwords or PII.
Enforce HTTPS everywhere to protect JWTs in transit and prevent token leakage.
Add rate limiting and circuit breakers to absorb 5xx spikes during the migration rollout.

Deploy JWT validation as middleware behind a load balancer and run a canary release before full rollout.
During migration, support both session cookies and JWTs so existing clients continue working.
Issue short‑lived JWTs (e.g., 15 min) with refresh tokens to limit exposure if a token is stolen.
Sign JWTs with a strong algorithm (e.g., RS256) and rotate signing keys regularly.
Never store sensitive data (passwords, PII) in JWT payloads; keep claims minimal.
Validate token expiration, audience, and issuer on every request to prevent replay attacks.
Monitor 5xx spikes; use a circuit‑breaker pattern to isolate failing services and protect the DB.

Deploy JWTs alongside session cookies using a feature flag for gradual rollout.
Issue short‑lived JWTs (e.g., 5‑15 min) and rotate signing keys regularly.
Store JWT signing keys in a secure vault or HSM; never hard‑code them.
Validate JWT signature, issuer, audience, expiration, and required claims on every request.
Serve all auth traffic over HTTPS; set Secure and HttpOnly on fallback cookies.
Do not embed sensitive PII in JWT payloads; use opaque user IDs instead.
Implement revocation via a token blacklist or by using very short TTLs.
